# Gerritsen's Color Wheel — How Our Eyes Create Color from Spectral Imbalance

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube Shorts)
**Date:** 2024-12-21
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/shorts/49k6jJOH3Pc
**Views:** 3,866

## Description

Gerritsen's 1975 acetate overlay color wheel diagram — one of Color Nerd's all-time favorites — visually demonstrates how all hues arise from different spectral absorptions interacting with our three cone types.

## Transcript

I'm going to show you one of my favorite ever color wheel diagrams. It's in this book by Frans Gerritsen from 1975. He uses these dots — blue, green, red — to symbolize the three different areas of sensitivity of the human eye.

A yellow pigment absorbs short wavelengths and reflects longer wavelengths. This is what a magenta pigment does — it absorbs the middle wavelengths. And a cyan pigment absorbs only the long wavelengths, right?

These are printed on like acetate so you can see through. And on this page you're seeing that the yellow half of the circle — the short wavelengths are being absorbed on this side. The half of the circle is white because all three areas of the spectrum are coming through.

When you overlay the second page, you get red out of yellow and magenta, because the red is a combination of two absorptions, right? The yellow is absorbing the short wavelengths and the magenta is absorbing the middle wavelengths. Red is created when both are absorbed.

And then finally we overlay this sheet with cyan and we get the whole picture. He follows it up with this like more elaborated one, which is showing you that all these different hues around the hue circle are just the cause of different parts of the spectrum coming through to our three sensors in our eye.

And I think this is such a beautiful and perfect and simple explanation of how color perception works.

## Key Concepts

- **Three cone sensitivities** represented as blue, green, red dots
- **Subtractive color as spectral absorption:**
  - Yellow pigment → absorbs short wavelengths (blue cone region)
  - Magenta pigment → absorbs middle wavelengths (green cone region)
  - Cyan pigment → absorbs long wavelengths (red cone region)
- **Acetate overlay system:** each transparent sheet subtracts one region of the spectrum; overlaying them builds up the full hue circle through progressive absorption
- **Yellow + Magenta = Red** (short + mid wavelengths absorbed → only long wavelengths reach the eye)
- **All hues = different spectral imbalances** reaching our three cone types
- Connects subtractive mixing directly to cone physiology — not just "paint mixing rules"
